examples.title: Producer-Consumer with Semaphores
examples.description: Bounded buffer solution using counting and binary semaphores
examples.explanation: The solution uses three semaphores: empty (tracks empty slots, init N), full (tracks filled slots, init 0), and mutex (binary semaphore for mutual exclusion). Producer waits for empty slot, adds item, signals full. Consumer waits for full item, removes item, signals empty. The mutex ensures only one process accesses buffer at a time.

examples.title: Dining Philosophers - Asymmetric Solution
examples.description: Solution that prevents deadlock by breaking symmetry
examples.explanation: This solution breaks circular wait by having even-numbered philosophers pick up the right fork first, while odd-numbered pick up the left fork first. This breaks the symmetric deadlock scenario where all philosophers would simultaneously pick up their left forks. The solution prevents deadlock but may still allow starvation in unfair scheduling.

examples.title: Readers-Writers Problem - Writer Priority
examples.description: Solution giving priority to writers to prevent writer starvation
examples.code:
"""Readers-Writers Problem - Writer Priority Solution"""
import threading
import time

# Shared resources
readers_count = 0
write_request = False

# Semaphores
readers_mutex = threading.Semaphore(1)  # Protects readers_count
writers_mutex = threading.Semaphore(1)   # For writers
queue = threading.Semaphore(1)           # Ensures FIFO ordering

def reader(reader_id):
    global readers_count, write_request
    
    while True:
        queue.acquire()
        readers_mutex.acquire()
        
        if readers_count == 0:
            writers_mutex.acquire()
        
        readers_count += 1
        queue.release()
        readers_mutex.acquire()
        
        # Reading section
        print(f"Reader {reader_id} is reading. Count: {readers_count}")
        time.sleep(1)
        
        readers_mutex.acquire()
        readers_count -= 1
        
        if readers_count == 0:
            writers_mutex.release()
        
        readers_mutex.release()
        time.sleep(1)

def writer(writer_id):
    global write_request
    
    while True:
        queue.acquire()
        writers_mutex.acquire()
        queue.release()
        
        # Writing section
        print(f"Writer {writer_id} is writing.")
        time.sleep(1)
        
        writers_mutex.release()
        time.sleep(2)

# Create reader and writer threads
readers = [threading.Thread(target=reader, args=(i,)) for i in range(3)]
writers = [threading.Thread(target=writer, args=(i,)) for i in range(2)]

for t in readers + writers:
    t.start()

for t in readers + writers:
    t.join()
examples.explanation: This writer-priority solution ensures writers don't starve. The queue semaphore ensures FIFO ordering. When a writer is waiting, new readers must acquire the queue lock and will be blocked, allowing the waiting writer to proceed. The first reader acquires writers_mutex, subsequent readers just increment count. The last reader releases writers_mutex. Writers acquire both queue (for ordering) and writers_mutex (for exclusive access).
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for reader/writer entry and exit
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) for counter variables and semaphores

examples.title: Sleeping Barber Problem
examples.description: Classic sleeping barber implementation in Java
examples.explanation: This implementation uses three semaphores: customers (counts waiting customers, init 0), barber (signals barber is ready, init 0), and mutex (protects waiting counter). When a customer arrives, they check for empty chairs. If available, they increment waiting and signal customers. They then wait on barber semaphore. The barber waits on customers semaphore, decrements waiting, and signals barber semaphore. The customer then proceeds to get haircut. If all chairs full, customer leaves.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for customer arrival and service operations
examples.spaceComplexity: O(N) for chair capacity (waiting variable and semaphore queue)
